summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Assessment of contributions – relatively short relationship – Where the husband had intermingled/messy financial transactions with family members in business ventures – Final property adjustment orders made that achieve justice and equity to both parties

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

INTRODUCTION

This cross cultural union between the Country L father, Mr Dirksen (who in these Reasons I shall call “the husband”) and the Country F mother, Ms Sobol (who in these Reasons I shall call “the wife”), was established on a less than solid foundation, to some extent created by the wife’s desire to work, live and study in Australia.
As these Reasons reveal, the parties never, it seems, communicated effectively and were very different people:
the wife a highly educated and articulate woman with high expectations;
the husband an extremely hard-working tradesman who, with his brothers, had a successful business and also, like the wife, a close family and strong values; and
even the birth of their cherished child, X in 2016, did not seem to bring them closer, with very different parenting styles evident.
It, in hindsight, was not surprising that the parties separated under the one roof in August 2020 and physically separated 12 months later – a relationship, until then after their marriage, of six years.
The litigation began in January 2022; resulted in a bifurcated trial held in November 2024 relating to parenting issues, with the property dispute going to trial in February 2025.
Although the husband’s brother, and the co-owner now of the husband’s two heavily mortgaged properties, was a party, a consent Order made on 18 November 2024 enabled an agreement to be perfected releasing the brother Mr J, and the husband and wife could then concentrate on their property proceedings.
As these Reasons identify, ultimately conflicts about what interests should be included or excluded in the pool of interests occupied most of the trial time and submissions. The progress of the matter after November 2024 was made more difficult for the husband when he could no longer afford legal representation and appeared at the final property hearing unrepresented. The wife throughout was ably represented by solicitors and independent Counsel Ms Petrie.
Sadly, my ultimate findings reveal a modest pool of interests of just over $1,200,000 which may not have justified the level of costs these parties incurred, mostly I would find arising from their emotional perspectives which shaped this litigation.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

The husband commenced proceedings in Division 2 of this Court on 21 January 2022 (seeking at that stage parenting orders only). Property proceedings were introduced by the wife in her amended Response filed 2 February 2023, to which the husband responded by way of Reply filed 24 February 2023.
By Application in a Proceeding filed 17 February 2023, the wife sought, inter alia, spouse maintenance; that the husband provide or return a motor vehicle for the wife’s use; and orders restraining the husband from disposing of any interest he held in any property.
On 27 February 2023, a Senior Judicial Registrar made interim Orders by consent restraining the husband from dealing with two real properties at M Street, suburb N, New South Wales (“the suburb N property”) and O Street, suburb C, New South Wales (“the suburb C property”) subject to the wife providing an undertaking as to damages. The husband also consented to an order that he pay the wife’s costs of $2,000 within 42 days of the making of final property adjustment orders.
After the proceedings were transferred to Division 1 of this Court on 20 October 2023, the matter first came before me on 14 March 2024, at which time I ordered the parties to file a balance sheet, and other orders were made to progress the parenting matters.
By amended Initiating Application and Application in a Proceeding filed by the wife on 5 April 2024, the wife sought:
(a) a declaration pursuant to s 106B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) that the transfer of the husband’s interest in the suburb N property is void and that the transfer be set aside;

(b) a declaration that the husband’s brother, Mr J, holds his share of the suburb N property on trust for the husband;

(c) that the suburb N and suburb C properties be sold to effect a property distribution; and

(d) Mr J be joined to the proceedings and that he be restrained from further dealing with the suburb N and suburb C properties.

By Order made 19 April 2024, Mr J was joined to the proceedings as the Second Respondent. The parties were also ordered on this date to file further response material, and the property and parenting proceedings were set down for final hearing before me for three days commencing 30 September 2024 in Sydney.
After Replies were filed by the husband and his brother, the matter returned before me on 17 June 2024. The Court was advised on this occasion that the parties were scheduled to attend mediation in August 2024 in relation to property matters. To allow the parties to focus on the mediation process and for the Court to list another trial, the three-day final hearing originally listed in late September 2024 was adjourned to commence on 18 November 2024, for five days. The following Orders were made to progress the property matters:
That the Second Respondent shall, within twenty one (21) days, provide to the Applicant husband and the First Respondent wife a schedule of payments including dates and amounts he says he has loaned to the husband referred to in his affidavit, together with supporting documents evidencing the source of the loans was him.
That within seven (7) days thereafter, the husband shall file and serve by affidavit confirming:
the loans asserted by the Second Respondent were loans actually made;
any repayments he asserts he has made off those loans;
any conditions relating to the loans with respect to repayment or interest; and
how the funds he received were used.
That by no later than 4.00pm on 29 July 2024, the wife file and serve points of claim where she clearly articulates the basis of claim against the Second Respondent.
That within fourteen (14) days from the date of this Order, the wife shall provide all documents in her possession, power and control in relation to the request for discovery by the husband dated 18 April 2024.
Unfortunately, mediation did not occur due to a cancellation by the mediator, and mediation was postponed to late September 2024.
The matter again returned to my list on 5 September 2024 after the wife filed her Points of Claim on 31 July 2024, as directed, albeit late. Orders were made on this occasion for the husband and Mr J to each file a Defence to the wife’s Points of Claim, and directions were made in relation to the payment of real property valuers’ fees.
When the trial commenced before me on 18 November 2024, the husband was represented by Ms Wilson of Counsel, the wife was represented by Ms Petrie of Counsel and Mr May of Counsel represented the Second Respondent. Having been stood down for better part of the day, the Court was presented with proposed final consent orders, which were made by me, to conclude the Second Respondent’s involvement in the property proceedings, namely:
That the Second Respondent acknowledges that he owes the Applicant the sum of $257,909.00 with such amount to be paid into an interest bearing controlled monies account opened by York Law by 31 March 2025 with [Mr BB] and [Mr CC] being signatories, failing which the [suburb C] property be sold and the Second Respondent’s liability under this order (the sum of $257,909.00) be paid from his 50% share of the [suburb C] property together with interest in accordance with the Rules from the date of default.
That the parties agree that the payments by the Second Respondent in Order 1 hereof are in full and final satisfaction of all monies owing by the Second Respondent to the Applicant.
That in the substantive proceedings between the Applicant and First Respondent, the parties agree that in the event that the Court intends to make an order for the sale of either the [suburb N] property or the [suburb C] property, then the Second Respondent has the first option to buy the Applicant’s interest in either or both properties on the following terms:
That the market value of the property will be as per the single expert reports obtained for trial being those dated 10 September 2024 and prepared by Knight Frank and if Knight Frank varies the valuation of [suburb N], then the amended valuation shall apply for the [suburb N] property, or the market value as found by the Court;
The Second Respondent will buy the Applicant's interest in each or both properties and make payment within four months from date of final orders to issue;
The payment to be made by the Second Respondent to the Applicant or to any other person as per any final orders as to disbursement of proceeds will be calculated as per the formula below:
SRO = (V- SC-M-A)/2 – ASE – CGT

Where:

SRO = Second Respondent's obligated amount

V = Value of property as per single expert report prepared by Knight Frank dated 10 September 2024 or as amended for the suburb N property

SC = Selling costs including legal fees incurred on sale

M = Mortgage balance at time of settlement together with any and all breakfee and costs of refinance imposed by Commonwealth Bank of Australia as outgoing mortgagee as well as all fees and charges to be imposed by the incoming mortgagee

A = Adjustments on settlement for land tax, council rates and water rates

ASE = Applicant’s share of expenses and outgoings paid by the Second Respondent on behalf of the Applicant from 31 October 2024 to date of settlement;

CGT = The notional Capital Gains Tax (CGT) to be paid by the Applicant to the ATO on sale of his 50% interest in the property in question, with such monies to be placed in the Applicant's accountant’s trust account.
That the proceedings against the Second Respondent be dismissed and he be removed as a party to the proceedings.
That pending final Orders in relation to the property proceedings, the Applicant and Second Respondent be restrained from further encumbering or otherwise dealing with the [suburb C] and [suburb N] properties.
That as between the First Respondent and Second Respondent costs be reserved and determined after the substantive proceedings between Applicant and First Respondent are concluded.
It is also relevant to record that the following notations were made to the Orders of 18November 2024:
A. That the parties agree:
That as at October 2022, the Second Respondent was owed money by the Applicant which monies he, the Second Respondent, advanced to the Applicant from about mid-2021.
In about October 2022, the Applicant sold 50% of his interest in the property at [suburb N] to the Second Respondent for consideration of $1,000,000 (for the 50%) and at the same time the Second Respondent assumed responsibility for 50% of the mortgage owing by the Applicant at the time. At the time, the Applicant's net equity in the [suburb N] property was circa $850,000 and the 50% was worth about $425,000. At the time, the Applicant owed the Second Respondent some $250,000. The Applicant and Second Respondent agreed and the Second Respondent promised to continue to advance funds to the Applicant to enable the Applicant to meet his future expenses and the same be in reduction of what the Second Respondent owes the Applicant after purchase which has occurred.
In or about mid-2022, the Supreme Court appointed a trustee at the request of one [Mr P] to sell the [suburb C] property, which property was owned at the time by the Applicant and his brother [Mr P]. After the trustee was appointed, the Second Respondent negotiated with the trustee and bought [Mr P’s] 50% interest for $2,125,000 (as to the 50%) less 50% of the mortgage secured over the property at the time. At the time the mortgage owing to Q Bank was approximately $1,941,240.34.
To enable him to pay his 50% share of the net equity, the Second Respondent partly funded the purchase and with consent of the Applicant, partly from increased borrowing. The Second Respondent's share of the new mortgage negotiated with Commonwealth Bank of Australia was about $980,000.
From settlement in about early December 2022 to date, the Second Respondent met his share of the interest on the $980,000; continued to advance funds to the Applicant to enable him to meet his expenses such that as at 31 October 2024, the Applicant owed the Second Respondent $723,088.82. Having regard to the increased borrowing being a joint liability of the Applicant and Second Respondent, the effect of what the Second Respondent owes the Applicant on [suburb C] ($980,998.37) as against what the Applicant owes the Second Respondent ($723,088.82), the Second Respondent owes the Applicant the sum of $257,099, which sum the parties have agreed to pay into a controlled monies account pending this Honourable Court’s determination of the financial proceedings between the Applicant and First Respondent.
On the morning of the second day of the final hearing (19 November 2024), the parties informed the Court that an in-principal agreement had been reached to resolve the property dispute, but that parenting issues remained unresolved. Cross examination in relation to parenting matters then proceeded, with the Applicant husband being cross-examined by Counsel for the Respondent wife until the luncheon adjournment. Upon the trial resuming after the luncheon adjournment, the Court was informed that the in-principal property agreement had broken down. At this stage, it was clear that the trial on both parenting and property issues could not conclude in the remaining allocated trial time, with the matter having been stood down for the most part of day one for the parties to negotiate a final agreement, at least concerning Mr Dirksen. Accordingly, the trial proceeded in relation to parenting issues only, with the property dispute to be set down for a further final hearing at a later stage. Judgment in relation to parenting matters was reserved at the conclusion of day four of the hearing (21 November 2024), and the property trial was listed for two days commencing 13 February 2025.
On 4 December 2024, the Court made final parenting Orders for the Reasons delivered the same day (see Dirksen & Sobol [2024] FedCFamC1F 831). Those Orders provided for, inter alia:
(a) the parents to have joint decision making responsibility;

(b) X to live with the mother and spend time with the father:

(i) for 2025, each alternate weekend from after school Thursday to before school Monday and overnight on Thursday in the off week during school term;

(ii) from 2026, each alternate week from after school Thursday to before school Tuesday;

(iii) half of the school term holidays, with special provision for the end of year school holiday period and other special occasions; and

(iv) by telephone each Sunday and Tuesday when not in the father’s care.

Less than a month before the property trial was scheduled to commence, the husband’s legal representative filed a Notice of Ceasing to Act on 17 January 2025. It was therefore unsurprising that, at the commencement of the hearing on 13 February 2025, the husband made an oral application for an adjournment of the trial. For reasons given orally that day, the adjournment application was refused and the trial proceeded on 13 and 14 February 2025.
The only witnesses cross-examined were the husband, the wife and the wife’s father, Mr R. At the conclusion of the evidence, the wife’s Counsel gave oral submissions, a transcript of which was obtained by the Court and provided to the parties, to assist the husband in preparing and filing written submissions, which he did on 23 April 2025.
On 14 February 2025, the Court also made an order by consent for the wife to receive, as an interim property distribution, the sum of $257,909 from the funds due to be paid to her solicitor’s trust account by the husband’s brother, Mr J.

INITIAL PRELIMINARY POINT

It was not until the husband’s written submissions were filed on 23 April 2025 that he raised a preliminary issue.
I say “he”, as although there is no indication on the document that the author of the submissions was a lawyer and not the husband, the comments at paragraph 2 and what followed at paragraphs 3 to 6 are concerning where:
(a) the husband is seeking leave to re-open for the limited purpose of tendering documents;

(b) no application was formally filed and there is no evidence the wife is even aware of the “application” – especially where no submissions in reply to those of the husband have been filed; and

(c) there is not, in my assessment applying the standard principles (see for example Summitt & Summitt & Ors (Re-opening) [2009] FamCA 365; EB v CT (No. 2) [2008] QSC 306; Reid v Brett [2005] VSC 18), a basis to re-open simply because the “attachments” to the husband’s trial affidavit were not formally tendered as a result of the husband being self-represented.

I have considered this issue – albeit without submissions from the wife – and having conducted the hearing where both parties relied upon a binder of “attachments”, few of which were referred to, and where the Court did not of its own initiative at anytime raise the need to comply with r 8.15(3)(e) (namely to tender attachments formally), and no attempt by the legal representatives of the wife sought to do so.
In the circumstances, I have decided not to formally “re-open” but without strict compliance with r 8.15(3)(e), I will permit both parties to rely on the attachments to their affidavits.
In circumstances where all submissions are completed, neither party can be taken by surprise. I shall remain a little concerned by the wife’s election not to seek to respond or reply in any way to the husband’s written submissions, but my pending retirement encouraged me to forge on and deliver these Reasons.

BRIEF CONTEXTUAL HISTORY

This history is directed to relevant events in the property proceedings and does not engage generally in the parenting proceedings, which, as I have already noted, were the subject of a contested final hearing in November 2024 and final parenting Orders made by me on 4 December 2024 (see Dirksen & Sobol [2024] FedCFamC1F 831).
Statements of fact hereafter should be construed as findings of fact.
The husband is now aged 54 years and the wife is aged 41 years. The wife was born in Country F. The parties met in 2011 when the wife was in Australia studying. The wife returned to Country F in 2012 after she obtained her MBA. The parties went on to be engaged in or around September 2013 and travelled between Country F and Australia to visit one another before the wife permanently relocated to Australia in 2014 and took up residence with the husband in the suburb N property.
The parties married three months later in 2014 and were blessed with the birth of their only child, X, who is now nine years of age. X has some additional challenging behaviours, having been diagnosed with ASD and ADHD in 2023.
In late 2019, the parties moved out of the suburb N property and into rental accommodation at suburb S so that a new house could be built on the suburb N property. At the time of the trial in February 2025, the husband was living in the suburb S unit with his parents. Tensions grew in the parties’ relationship which led to their separation under one roof from August 2020 after an incident resulting in a protection order being made against the husband and a charge of common assault, with final physical separation occurring in August 2021 according to the wife when she moved out of the home with the child (and the maternal grandmother). The parties were ultimately divorced in 2023.
Rather than set out now all the transactions that took place during this relatively short relationship, I will deal with various events when making findings about the items in the balance sheet, and when discussing contributions and any relevant s 75(2) factors.

STATUTORY PATHWAY

As the trial was completed before the June 2025 amendments to the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act’), the statutory pathway to be applied can be concisely explained as follows.
Shortly stated, but more concisely and elaborately described in the Full Court decision in Hickey & Hickey [2003] FamCA 395; (2003) FLC 93-143, in a property settlement case, the Court must adopt a well-known four-step process, essentially:
(a) to identify the pool of assets and liabilities generally, and usually at the time of hearing;

(b) to assess the relative contributions of both the financial, non-financial, direct and indirect nature as specified by s 79(4);

(c) to consider the factors as are relevant contained in s 75(2) of the Act; and

(d) finally, consider the ultimate analysis to determine whether the order the Court proposes to make is just and equitable to both parties.

Both parties contend, and I agree, that it is just and equitable within the meaning of s 79(2) of the Act to make property alteration orders.

POOL OF INTERESTS

As Exhibit 23 reveals, the draft balance sheet tendered by the wife and the subject of submissions by the parties as I set out below, had many areas of agreement. Further concessions to Exhibit 23 entities were made.
As the discussion which follows identifies, the final submissions mostly related to liabilities and addbacks. Although many items in Exhibit 23 were conceded as properly now excluded, I have maintained for disputed items, the numbering revealed in Exhibit 23.
I further remind the parties that where the written submissions of the husband were not the subject of response by the wife, I am restricted in determining some issues from just the oral submissions of Ms Petrie for the wife (tested to some degree by the Bench) and the written submissions of the husband. This is not entirely satisfactory from the Court’s perspective.

Item 8

Although the husband said that the figure for the offset account at 18 November 2024 should not change, the wife’s Counsel asserted that the balance of the offset account was more like $50,000 – apparently because of some document the husband brought to the Court on 13 February 2025. No document was tendered and the husband’s submissions make no such concession. In the circumstances, I adopt a balance of $25,998 for the Commonwealth Bank offset account.

Items 11 and 30

These items relate to the motor vehicle 1 purchased, with finance by the wife after her car was repossessed post separation. I see no unfairness to either party in excluding items 11 and 30.

Item 12

It is acknowledged that the funds payable by the husband’s brother Mr J pursuant Order 1 made by consent on 18 November 2024 totalling $257,909 were paid to wife’s solicitors by agreement, and as ordered on 14 February 2025.

Items 20 and 37

These items relate to transactions and liabilities associated with the suburb N property. Whilst the parties agreed, for the purpose of the balance sheet, that the market value of the husband’s 50% interest is $1,050,000, a dispute exists, not that 50% of the current mortgage liability (attaching to that interest) is $675,000 but, as the wife contends, the “true debt” is less and therefore the husband’s “equity” in the property is greater than $375,000. The wife argues that either the Court should “add back” as some premature disposition (in the Townsend & Townsend [1994] FamCA 144; (1994) 18 Fam LR 505 sense) a sum the husband has benefited from relating to the increased borrowings and/or payments made in some way by his brother, Mr J. The wife calculates the “add back” to be $246,150. If this was accepted, either as an add back or as a notional reduction in the debt liability of the husband, the effect is to increase the pool (and his notional equity) by $246,150.
Considering the agreed notations to the Order of 18 November 2022 (notations (a), (b) and (c)), that recorded that at October 2022 the husband’s net equity was approximately $425,000 and is now $375,000, this bare example does not support an “add back” of $246,150. Furthermore, in circumstances where the parties have full particulars of monies loaned to the husband by his brother; agreeing after negotiation that allowing for the equity the brother Mr J secured in the suburb N property without payment at the time of transfer, but adjusting for a myriad of other matters (only known to the parties), to come to a figure of $257,909 means, in my view, no “add back” for the suburb N property or reduction in the loan can be justified. I will as a result exclude item 37.

Items 21, 38 and 42

These items relate to the transactions and liabilities associated with the suburb C property, and reveal a quite “messy” transaction involving the husband’s other brother, Mr P; his brother Mr J and proceedings in the Supreme Court of New South Wales.
In the notations to the 18 November 2022 consent Order earlier referred to at (c), (d) and (e) some particulars about the transaction relating to the suburb C property are agreed to by the parties – including the wife. The gravamen of the transaction is that Mr J partly funded the purchase of 50% in the suburb C property, by increasing the loans over the property, with the consent of the husband – so that Mr J could purchase Mr P’s 50% share for $2,125,000 from the Trustee appointed by the Supreme Court of New South Wales. The sum payable was reduced by $970,620 – being 50% of the then mortgage owing to Q Bank which was refinanced.
Notation (e) acknowledges that at settlement and from about early December 2022, Mr J met his share of the interest of $980,000; continued to advance funds to the husband; such that by 31 October 2024, the husband owed the brother Mr J $723,088. This debt was offset against liability share that increased from the joint borrowings ($980,998) – which created the balancing payment of $257,909 earlier referred to.
I acknowledge that Ms Petrie did her best to try unravel these intermingled financial transactions in circumstances where she said the husband did not make full and timely disclosure. No reconciliation as to how income from rentals, payments for expenses and how (and why) seemingly the offset account has not increased was provided.
However, considering the written submissions of the husband at paragraphs 8 to 12 (mortgage over the suburb C property); at paragraphs 13 to 15 (acquisition of the suburb C property) and paragraphs 16 to 21 (the acquisition of the suburb N property by Mr J), which I broadly accept persuade me that “adding back” these sums (or otherwise notionally reducing current mortgage liabilities), is not justified. I again remind the parties that to do so is, in any event, the “exception to the rule” (see Trevi & Trevi [2018] FamCAFC 173).
At paragraphs 22 to 23 of the husband’s submissions, consistent with Chorn & Hopkins [2004] FamCA 633; (2004) FLC 93-204, the husband concedes that of the current loans it is fair to accept some of his legal fees were paid totalling $114,170. That sum I find should be “added back”.
The wife’s assertion that $844,000 is said, at item 42, to be “equity spent from transfer of the [suburb C] property to [Mr J]”, similarly fails as set out.
To further support my exercise of discretion in this respect, the total of “add backs” of $2,073,008 – standing back in the financial history and circumstances, simply has insufficient foundation.

Items 22 and 31

These items relate to the entity now in liquidation, named T Pty Ltd. Some background is helpful, taken form both the liquidator’s report dated 19 April 2024 and the husband’s evidence and cross-examination.
The company, which formally traded as T Service operated in the building industry from around 2009 (although an earlier entity operated by Mr P and the husband had commenced trading 25 years ago). At the time of liquidation on 20 November 2023, the husband was the sole director and shareholder. The liquidator’s report speaks for itself and no evidence from the liquidator was offered nor was the liquidator the subject of cross-examination as to the opinions expressed in the report, including:
(a) the significant drop in trading income related to Mr P breaking away from the business to commence his own operations, and he also procured the company’s assets leaving the company without crucial assets to operate the business;

(b) non-current receivables were made up of loans recorded as being:

Mr P
	
$695,964

Mr Dirksen
	
$453,075

Mr J
	
$89,833

	
($1,238,782)
(c) the largest creditor is the Australian Taxation Office (“ATO”) who had lodged a proof of debt for $992,024;

(d) the liquidator was reviewing options as to whether to continue legal proceedings commenced by the company against Mr P for return of assets and/or damages;

(e) the liquidator confirmed loans had been taken from the company and he was investigating whether the loans “amount to unreasonable director related transactions”;

(f) in circumstances where, despite a report to creditors on 28 November 2023 where the liquidator informed the creditors of his estimate of fees to “complete the matter”, no monies or recovery of assets had been either received or achieved by the time of the hearing.

In these circumstances, the only basis to include a loan to the liquidated entity of $453,075, in my view, is if I were satisfied the liquidator has the funds and intention to seek to recover the repayment. As noted, a larger loan is recorded as being owed by Mr P. If the loans were recovered, then the ATO would receive payment. There is no suggestion on the evidence before me that the liquidator will take any action. Furthermore, where the loans may be reasonable, the level of liability is uncertain. As a result, item 31 in the balance sheet will be excluded. I have not ignored Exhibit 40 however those emails alone do not persuade me to include the debt.
Despite the considered submissions of Ms Petrie, the responding submissions of the husband at paragraphs 24; 24.1; 24.2; 24.3 and 25 to 29 give the debt an entirely different character than that submitted by Ms Petrie for the wife and the description of item 22 in Exhibit 23. I do not in any way suggest that Counsel tried to mislead the Court, but until the written submissions of the husband drew my attention to his affidavit (paragraph 142) and the exhibits to the husband’s affidavit at pages 192 to 199, my discussion with Counsel for the wife was founded on an understanding these liabilities were debts of the liquidated company. I agree with the submissions on behalf of the husband that it could not be contended the ATO will not enforce the debts as it is a personal obligation of the husband. Whilst I accept, if proceedings were to be commenced by the ATO, that some compromise of the debt might be negotiated, as the evidence currently stands, I regard it as just and equitable to include item 22.
For completeness, whilst the husband may have incurred a loan liability to the entity T Pty Ltd and guaranteed some repayments to external accountants U accountancy service, I am not persuaded on the evidence it is just and equitable to include liabilities at items 32, 33, 34 and 35 which seem to have arisen well post separation.

Item 26 debt for rental

I note the husband sensibly does not seek to press for the inclusion of this debt in the balance sheet.

Item 27 land tax

After referring to part of the cross-examination by Counsel for the wife, the husband contends he “was not challenged in cross-examination” on the claim for payment of land tax. The submissions refer to his affidavit at paragraph 164, where he says he did not pay his half share of the land tax owing when the transfer between Mr P and Mr J occurred – and he was not required to pay his share as part of the transaction.
In circumstances where significant obligations of the husband have been funded by Mr J, and where significant intermingling of funds has occurred, which are incapable of reconciliation, I am not satisfied it is fair to identify just one charge attaching to the property. Rents have been available. Why the husband has chosen not to pay a clearly post-separation liability – apart from claiming he is unable to do so – is somewhat of a mystery. I do not regard it as just and equitable to include the debt.

Item 36 monies paid by the wife to her parents

The wife gave a believable history of how her parents, considering a move to Australia from Country F, caused funds to be deposited in Australia into accounts initially operated by the wife and then transferred to other investments (for example a term deposit) before ultimately returning all the funds to her parents.
Her version was corroborated by the evidence in the affidavit of the wife’s father Mr R sworn 4 November 2024. Mr R was briefly cross-examined but did not waiver in his evidence. I regarded him as a truthful witness – despite the father’s beliefs and his written submissions. Item 36 will not be included in the balance sheet as an “add back” for the wife’s benefit.

Items 39 and 40 motor vehicle 2

In circumstances where I accept the timing of the husband’s transfer of registration in 2018 of these vehicles to his parents – he says to repay a debt – raises questions, I have no evidence of their current value. I have no evidence either from his parents – noting his mother Ms DD did give evidence in the parenting part of this dispute. If a debt was owing to the parents by the husband and that is why the cars were transferred, then I find it would have been possible to have at least the husband’s mother swear to that version. In the circumstances, I find that his mother (at least) was not likely to give evidence to assist him (Jones & Dunkel [1959] HCA 8; (1959) 101 CLR 298).
However, this does not mean the “value” of the cars can be inserted into the balance sheet. Even saying that, as is possible, the benefit given away by the husband is an issue to be considered pursuant to s 75(2)(o) is somewhat meaningless without evidence of value. I decide, on balance, to exclude the items from the balance sheet but accept, to a very minimal extent it might be a consideration under s 75(2)(e).
Based on these findings, I find the pool of interests of the parties to be as follows:

Husband

ADD BACKS

CONTRIBUTIONS

Both parties acknowledge that the husband’s initial contributions were superior to those of the wife, and I identify the evidence given and not seriously challenged that the parties introduced:
(a) the husband:
(i) equity in the suburb N property – he says of approximately $517,000;

(ii) superannuation of $130,000;

(iii) savings of $20,300;

(iv) motor vehicles, furniture and effects; and

(v) interest in business.

(b) the wife:

(i) Country F unit.
The husband’s superannuation was preserved and forms now part of his member entitlement in the self-managed superannuation fund. On the evidence, it is difficult to trace whether the cars owned by the husband at cohabitation were the same as the two motor vehicles he transferred to his parents.
What is clear is that whatever equity the husband had in the suburb N property at cohabitation, was diluted in part by the transaction where the husband transferred a 50% interest to Mr J – but did not receive any initial payment at the time of the transfer. Rather, the husband received over time further financial assistance from Mr J. The husband’s current equity in the suburb N property is now even less than he “introduced” at $375,000.
Ms Petrie submitted that in the absence of any retrospective valuation of the husband’s interest in the suburb N property, the Court should be careful to accept the husband’s lay opinion. The husband’s submissions (at paragraph 63) reference Frederick & Frederick [2019] FamCAFC 87 at [37]–[39], namely:
... a person can give some evidence by way of admission as to the value of real estate owned by them which can be accorded weight, notwithstanding such weight might be very much less than the weight given to that of a professional valuer, for example. The ordinary common experience of people is that where they own only a few significant assets such as cars and houses, they are well aware of the purchase price and have some knowledge, obtained from a variety of sources, about their value.
In the circumstances, although I am not required to make a definitive calculation of the husband’s equity in the suburb N property at cohabitation, I am comfortable to use his estimate as a guide.
The wife’s property in Country F has remained intact since the commencement of the relationship and I am not satisfied that any Country F income received by the wife did more than meet holding expenses of the unit.
The husband at the time of cohabitation was generating an income from his business T Pty Ltd, however the liquidation report suggests that the husband extracted funds from the business by way of “loans” and the business did not pay income tax as required – resulting in the director penalty notice issuing from the ATO, as previously discussed. That is, the family had the benefit of the income – without paying income tax.
The wife, I find, worked in the business for a short period, but more importantly the husband attributed income to her in the financial statements of the business (beyond her personal exertion income), for the purpose of minimising personal tax he may have incurred.
The nett benefit to these parties arising from the husband’s property transactions with Mr J in respect of the suburb N and suburb C properties is very difficult to determine. Certainly I accept that one benefit has been to maintain and preserve the interest in the properties – which in a rising property market has allowed market driven capital gains to accrue. with the husband still seeking development application approval for the suburb N property, to combine with an adjoining property owned by his parents to develop an education facility, it may be in time that the husband’s current 50% interest will improve if the application is approved.
The wife’s direct financial contributions were minimal, however, I find that her contribution as homemaker and parent to X from his birth in 2016 were superior to those of the husband and continue to this day. I do not suggest the husband made no contribution to the household, rather that he was a hard-working parent and not always available to attend to household duties.
There is no evidence of any extraordinary financial contributions, gifts or inheritances that introduced funds into the family coffers, save for the financial assistance received by the wife from her parents to place a deposit of $84,000 on a future home for her.
In my assessment, the initial direct financial contributions by the husband, even in the shorter relationship in this case, must be weighed against the myriad of other contributions made. I also do not ignore that much of any equity the husband’s initial contribution that initially existed has been reduced through a number of the confusing transactions engaged in with Mr J – some caused by the other brother, Mr P, and through the Supreme Court proceedings.
In the circumstances, I assess the parties’ contribution based entitlements as being 55% to the husband and 45% to the wife – a differential of 10%, which on the pool found to exist of $1,206,948 is approximately $120,000.

SECTION 75(2) FACTORS

In final submissions, but I accept on an uncertain pool (which the wife asserted was as high as $3,738,234, whilst the Court found the pool to be $1,206,948), the wife contended for an adjustment for s 75(2) factors of between 5-10%. The husband’s submissions (at paragraph 96) contends that if the Court “makes orders based on the contribution based assessment he contends” (namely 60/40 in favour of the husband) then “there would be no warrant for further adjustment” for s 75(2) factors.
I make findings about the relevant factors as follows:
(a) The husband is 12 years older than the wife and there is no evidence of any health issues for either party;

(b) I accept both parties have the capacity for earning income. The husband has generated a reasonably high income in the building industry in the past, and although at the final hearing he says his salary was $2,000 per week gross (working in his brother’s business Z Pty Ltd), the payslips tendered at Exhibit 35 reveal in mid-2023, his gross salary was only $31,200 per annum. This is a confusing picture, however I am comfortably satisfied that the husband’s income is much higher than the wife. The wife’s Financial Statement filed 4 November 2024 showed her primary source of income as being government benefits;

(c) Although the wife holds tertiary qualifications and is certainly employable, since she has lived in Australia, she has not used those qualifications for higher level employment. The roles the parties undertook in the marriage meant the wife, as primary carer for the child, did not have the chance to develop work experience in Australia. The husband has an established earning capacity, and as I understood this evidence, was thinking of undertaking some TAFE courses to allow him to work in education – with an eye to developing a facility in the future;

(d) The Court is required to take into consideration the need to protect a parent who wishes to continue the role as parent – which is the wife’s desire. In that regard however I accept X is now nine years of age, and as he gets older the wife’s capacity to work longer hours is likely to increase;

(e) I take into account the nature of the orders under s 79 that the Court proposes to make. The wife will retain her Country F property, has a fully leased motor vehicle, and has already received a partial property payment of $257,909. She will receive a further cash payment from the husband, but after she discharges her legal costs and establishes a home to live in, she will have limited assets in Australia;

(f) Although the husband shares with his brother Mr J some significant mortgage liabilities, he still has equity in valuable real estate with potential for future development. With a higher income (and I find earning capacity than the wife), he has a platform from which to create further wealth. I do not ignore the tax liabilities I find the husband has to meet through the director penalty notice, but it seems he intends to meet those obligations over time, rather than sell any property he holds with Mr J; and

(g) The parties hold modest superannuation. As I will discuss below, I do not believe the husband should be able to meet his assessed payment to the wife, by requiring her to take a share of his superannuation benefit in the self-managed superannuation fund – a fund that may have compliance issues.

Considering these factors, I regard a 10% adjustment in the wife’s favour is appropriate – which on this pool computes to the husband paying to the wife an amount of $120,000.

WHAT ORDERS ACHIEVE JUSTICE AND EQUITY?

Although the examination of the evidence that has preceded this discussion might suggest that dividing the pool of interests as to 55% to the wife and 45% to the husband might achieve justice and equity – it is the form of orders which must be considered (and their effect) to satisfy the statutory requirements.
In that regard, the husband in his final submissions attached a minute of orders which provided in effect for:
(a) the husband to pay to the wife $43,000 within 60 days;

(b) the wife to take a superannuation split with a base amount of $100,000 from his member benefit in the Dirksen Super Fund; and

(c) otherwise, each party shall retain their property interests at this time and the parties provided indemnities.

The wife’s final form of minute was tendered and marked Exhibit 32 and effectively provided for:
(a) in addition to the payment of $257,909 which the wife has already received, the husband shall pay the wife an additional sum of $850,000;

(b) the husband is to have four months to make payment, failing which either the husband’s brother Mr J shall pay to the wife the sum of $850,000 or the suburb N property is to be sold;

(c) if a sale of the husband’s interest in suburb N property does not achieve an outcome where the wife obtains $850,000 then the suburb C property shall be effected;

(d) the husband shall receive, by way of a splitting order, a 100% interest in the wife’s member benefit in the Dirksen Super Fund; and

(e) further declarations and indemnities are to be made.

These outcomes were largely consistent with the husband’s indication to the bench during the trial, that he felt the wife should receive $400,000, although his orders break up that total payment as:
Partial property payment
	
$257,909

Superannuation split
	
$100,000

Cash payment
	
$43,000

	
$400,909
The wife’s proposed minute was based on her receiving approximately $1,100,000 broken up as:
Partial property payment
	
$257,909

Cash payment (through sale of property)
	
$850,000

	
$1,107,909
The parties’ proposals were based on how they submitted the pool of interests should be assessed.
Simply however, where the Court has determined the pool to be $1,206,948 – the wife’s proposed minute would amount to her receiving the entirety of the pool of assets. This is not just and equitable.
As already noted, the asserted pool of interests excluding over $2 million in add-backs and, also contrary to the wife’s submissions, the Court brought into the balance sheet the husband’s liability to the ATO, which the wife contended be excluded.
The husband’s minute, whereby the husband seeks the wife accept a superannuation split of $100,000, whilst reducing the cash he would have to find to payout the wife, would not be fair to the wife as, at her age, it would be many years before she could access the entitlements. The husband brought into the relationship around $130,000 of superannuation and has preserved it. In my view, he should retain his superannuation, which he may be able to access as early as age 60 years through a transition to retirement process.
Based on the pool of interests of $1,206,948 and with the husband taking the wife’s very small balance in the self-managed superannuation fund, his 45% amounts to $543,126 made up as follows:
Nett interests identified in the pool of interests
	
$672,858

Plus the wife’s superannuation in self-managed superannuation fund
	
$5,700

	
$678,558

Less payment to wife
	
$135,432

	
$543,126
The wife’s 55% interest amounts to $663,821 made up as follows:
Nett interests identified in the pool of interests
	
$534,090

Less self-managed superannuation fund interest
	
$5,700

	
$528,390

Plus payment by husband
	
$135,432

	
$663,822
I would round up the husband’s payment to$135,500, payable within 60 days. To this sum of $135,500 should be added the costs Order in the wife’s favour made on 27 February 2023 of $2,000.
The terms of the Court Order made on 18 November 2024 required the Court to allow the husband’s brother to be heard if orders to sell the properties at suburb N and/or suburb C are contemplated.
In my view, on all the evidence, and particularly the desire demonstrated from the husband’s evidence to:
(a) retain his interest in the suburb N property, which he brought into the relationship, and which was (at the final hearing), still subject to some litigation in the New South Wales Land and Environment Court; and

(b) having negotiated with his brother Mr J to acquire, and refinance so as to retain the suburb C property,

it is not reasonable, in my view, to anticipate the husband will be unable to pay the wife the sum of $137,500 without selling either of those properties.

Accordingly, in making the orders which appear at the commencement of these Reasons, which I say are just and equitable as between the husband and wife, I acknowledge the “right” of Mr J to be heard if the husband is unable or unwilling to pay to the wife the sum ordered.
Neither party leaves this expensive litigation in a strong financial position, but both have a base from which, with hard work and prudent commercial decisions, they can build on that base to try and secure a better long term financial position.
